title: Anthropic AI copyright lawsuit: Update as judge approves massive settlement and payout for authors
excerpt: The decision stems from a 2024 lawsuit in which a group of authors claimed their work was unfairly used to train Anthropic’s popular Claude chatbot.
content_html:
Anthropic will finally have to pay authors for pirating their books.

Most Read from Fast Company

  • 19 million eggs sold at Kroger and other stores recalled due to Salmonella fears: See a list of impacted products

  • Long John Silver's is closing stores: See a list of locations where the seafood restaurants have shuttered

  • These small houses in Omaha reimagine the starter home

On Monday, U.S. District Judge Araceli Martínez-Olguín approved a $1.5 billion AI copyright settlement. The payout is believed to be the largest ever for a U.S. copyright suit.

Companies like Facebook parent Meta Platforms and ChatGPT maker OpenAI have also faced copyright lawsuits from authors.

What does the Anthropic settlement say?

Each member of the class action lawsuit could receive between $200 and $150,000 per work, according to the decision. However, it estimates that the per-work payment will be about $3,000. 

The payout stems from a 2024 copyright infringement lawsuit filed by a group of authors led by Andrea Bartz, Charles Graeber, and Kirk Wallace Johnson.

It claimed that Anthropic had committed "large-scale theft" in choosing to use pirated copies of books to train its chatbot, Claude.

In June 2025, Judge William Alsup—now retired—ruled that AI could be trained on copyrighted books, but that Anthropic had accessed these books illegally, taking bootleg copies from online libraries. 

The case was set to go to trial in December and could have led to hundreds of billions of dollars in damages owed by Anthropic. Instead, the company chose to settle in September for $1.5 billion. 

Almost a year later, Judge Martínez-Olguín has taken over for Judge Alsup and approved the settlement. 

Fast Company reached out to Anthropic for comment.

Monday was a busy day for Judge Martínez-Olguín, as she also granted the temporary halt of the Paramount-Warner Bros. merger. California led a 12-state coalition that argued the merger will violate antitrust law.

This post originally appeared at fastcompany.com

content_html_zh:
Anthropic 最终将不得不向盗版书籍的作者付费。

大多数阅读来自《快公司》

  • 由于担心沙门氏菌，克罗格和其他商店出售的 1900 万个鸡蛋被召回：查看受影响产品列表
  • Long John Silver's 即将关闭商店：查看海鲜餐厅已关闭的地点列表
  • 奥马哈的这些小房子重新构想了入门之家

周一，美国地区法官 Araceli Martínez-Olguín 批准了一项价值 15 亿美元的人工智能版权和解协议。 此次赔偿被认为是美国版权诉讼有史以来金额最大的一次。

Facebook 母公司 Meta Platforms 和 ChatGPT 制造商 OpenAI 等公司也面临着作者的版权诉讼。

人择协议说了什么？

根据裁决，集体诉讼的每位成员每件作品可获得 200 至 15 万美元的赔偿。 不过，预计每件作品的报酬约为 3,000 美元。

这笔赔偿源于 2024 年由 Andrea Bartz、Charles Graeber 和 Kirk Wallace Johnson 领导的一组作者提起的版权侵权诉讼。

它声称 Anthropic 选择使用盗版书籍来训练其聊天机器人 Claude，犯下了“大规模盗窃罪”。

2025 年 6 月，现已退休的威廉·阿尔苏普法官裁定人工智能可以在受版权保护的书籍上进行训练，但 Anthropic 非法访问了这些书籍，从在线图书馆盗取了盗版副本。

该案定于 12 月开庭审理，可能导致 Anthropic 承担数千亿美元的损失。 相反，该公司于 9 月份选择以 15 亿美元和解。

近一年后，马丁内斯·奥尔金法官接替阿尔苏普法官并批准了和解方案。

Fast Company 联系了 Anthropic 征求意见。

周一对马丁内斯·奥尔金法官来说是忙碌的一天，因为她还批准了派拉蒙-华纳兄弟电影公司的暂停放映。 合并。 加州领导了一个由 12 个州组成的联盟，认为此次合并将违反反垄断法。
